ontology.foodon_gap_note: No Burmese-specific fried rice entry exists in FoodOn; closest parent 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002694) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXM covers generic fried rice; no Burmese chicken fried rice specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Burmese chicken fried rice.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Burmese chicken fried rice is approximately 300 g, typical of a single-serve restaurant portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice with Chicken — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central 'Fried rice, with meat' (SR Legacy ID 35188)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No Burmese-specific chicken fried rice entry exists in FAO/INFOODS SEA or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice with meat entries and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy 'Fried rice, with meat' (FDC ID 35188). Burmese-style fried rice uses similar ingredients (rice, chicken, egg, soy sauce, oil, aromatics) to regional analogues. Nutrient values represent a reasonable central estimate for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; fried rice analogous entry; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Burmese chicken fried rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'fried rice' (mixed/Asian style) reported at GI ~55 in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 67.5) / 100 = 37.1, based on 300 g serving with 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce, as a single serving can contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load per serving (~37) due to refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, pair it with non-starchy vegetables, and be aware that the high glycemic load per serving (~37) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat content with cholesterol from egg and chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption due to high sodium (~1140 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~3.3 g per serving), and choose preparations with less oil and reduced soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese chicken fried rice (ထမင်းကြော် ကြက်, romanized: Htamin Kyaw Kyat) is a popular Burmese stir-fried rice dish made by wok-frying cooked rice with diced or shredded chicken, egg, onion, garlic, soy sauce, and cooking oil, typically garnished with spring onion. The dish is a staple of Burmese home cooking and restaurant menus, including in Singapore's Burmese dining establishments. The texture is light and slightly chewy from the rice, with savory umami flavors from soy sauce and aromatics. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 489 kcal, 21.6 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 67.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high (~1140 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per serving is high (~37), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is dairy-free and typically halal when prepared with halal chicken. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Burmese fried rice differs from Chinese or Thai fried rice in its use of lighter seasoning and the characteristic Burmese preference for fish sauce or soy sauce as the primary condiment.

fried rice: ထမင်းကြော်

confidence_reason: No exact Burmese chicken fried rice entry was found in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice with meat entries and cross-validated with USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat. GI is estimated from the closest analogous fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the analogous adaptation rather than an exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice with meat and USDA SR Legacy 'Fried rice, with meat' (FDC ID 35188); no exact Burmese chicken fried rice entry found in approved primary sources. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish type. Trans fat is 0.0 as confirmed negligible in the source data for this dish type. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries; gi_category is 'low' per the Veritas threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 22.5 g carb per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 37.1. The cuisine_tags in the input ('Noodle', 'Lor Mee') appear to be erroneous metadata from the field survey seed record and are not reflected in this output, which correctly classifies the dish as a fried rice preparation. Burmese cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); the mandatory local-language name for this dish is Burmese script (ထမင်းကြော် ကြက်) and romanized form (Htamin Kyaw Kyat), which are provided.
